name: Linux Kernel
facts: Created by Linus Torvalds while he was a student at the University of Helsinki
Initially developed as a free alternative to the MINIX operating system
First announced on August 25, 1991, in a Usenet posting
Version 1.0 was released in March 1994, supporting only single-processor i386 systems

children.name: Clear Linux
facts: Developed by Intel specifically optimized for Intel hardware
Designed for high performance in cloud and container environments
Uses a unique bundle-based package management system
Implements aggressive performance optimizations and security mitigations

children.name: Solus
facts: Originally started as SolusOS (Debian-based) but was later rebuilt from scratch
Developed the Budgie desktop environment, which is now also available on other distributions
Uses the eopkg package management system derived from PiSi
Follows a curated rolling release model that balances stability and up-to-date software

children.name: SUSE Linux
facts: Originally a German translation of Slackware
Became the first distribution with a YaST graphical installer
Split into openSUSE (community) and SUSE Linux Enterprise (commercial) in 2005

children.name: openSUSE
facts: Community-driven version of SUSE Linux
Offers both Leap (regular release) and Tumbleweed (rolling release) versions
Known for its powerful YaST configuration tool and KDE integration

children.name: GeckoLinux
facts: Polished spins of openSUSE Leap and Tumbleweed with better out-of-the-box desktop experience
Adds multimedia codecs and proprietary drivers that openSUSE omits for licensing reasons
Available in multiple desktop editions including GNOME, Plasma, Xfce, Cinnamon, and Budgie
Name is a playful reference to the Gecko lizard, complementing openSUSE's chameleon mascot

children.name: openSUSE Aeon
facts: Immutable GNOME desktop spin from openSUSE
Built around transactional updates for a more stable desktop base

children.name: openSUSE Kalpa
facts: Immutable KDE Plasma desktop spin from openSUSE
Shares the transactional base model used by Aeon

children.name: openSUSE Slowroll
facts: openSUSE flavor that moves more slowly than Tumbleweed
Aims to offer a rolling model with slightly more conservative pacing

children.name: openSUSE Leap
facts: Stable openSUSE release line built for reliability and predictable updates
Shares its heritage with SUSE Linux Enterprise and the wider openSUSE ecosystem

children.name: openSUSE Tumbleweed
facts: Rolling openSUSE release line with continuously updated packages
Built for users who want the latest tested kernels, desktops, and drivers

children.name: openSUSE MicroOS
facts: Immutable openSUSE system designed for container workloads and automated administration
Uses transactional updates and rollback-friendly snapshots

children.name: openSUSE Leap Micro
facts: Lightweight immutable server OS built for containerized and virtualized workloads
Community version based on SUSE Linux Enterprise Micro technologies

children.name: Slackware
facts: One of the oldest Linux distributions still in active development
Known for its simplicity and UNIX-like design philosophy

children.name: Slax
facts: Designed to run from USB drives or CD-ROMs without installation
Uses a modular approach allowing easy customization

children.name: Salix
facts: Fully compatible with Slackware but more user-friendly
Follows a 'one application per task' philosophy to reduce bloat

children.name: Zenwalk
facts: Aims to be a modern, fast, and lightweight Slackware-based distribution
Optimized for desktop usage with a single application per task approach

children.name: VectorLinux
facts: Canadian Slackware-based distribution focused on fast performance on modest hardware
One of the earlier distros to offer a polished desktop experience on Slackware foundations
Developed by Robert Lange and Ladislav Bodnar (co-founder of DistroWatch)
Went through several iterations and is notable in Slackware community history

children.name: Absolute Linux
facts: Aims to be a complete, ready-to-use Slackware-based desktop distribution out of the box
Ships with IceWM as its default window manager for a fast, low-resource experience
Includes all the tools needed for daily use without requiring post-install configuration
Popular with users who appreciate Slackware's design but want a more ready-to-use setup

children.name: Parted Magic
facts: Created by Patrick Verner; one of the very few Slackware-based distributions to achieve widespread practical use outside the Slackware community
Designed as a live CD/USB bootable toolkit for disk partitioning, cloning, data recovery, and secure erasing — no installation required
In 2013 it became one of the rare open-source-based projects to successfully transition to a commercial pay-for-download model, still sold today
The name blends GNU Parted and the defunct PartitionMagic; the irony is that it outlived and outperformed the proprietary tool it was named after

children.name: Slackel
facts: Slackware-based distribution with a desktop-oriented focus
Known for lightweight desktop options such as MATE, KDE, and Openbox

children.name: Debian
facts: Known for its commitment to free software principles
Uses one of the most comprehensive package management systems (APT)

children.name: Deepin
facts: Developed in China with a focus on elegant design and user experience
Features the Deepin Desktop Environment (DDE)
Known for its custom applications and polished look

children.name: Parrot OS
facts: Designed for security experts, developers, and privacy-conscious users
Includes tools for penetration testing, forensics, and development
Lightweight and based on Debian

children.name: Ubuntu
facts: Named after the African philosophy of 'Ubuntu' meaning 'humanity to others'
Releases new versions every 6 months with Long Term Support (LTS) versions every 2 years

children.name: Linux Mint
facts: Known for its Cinnamon desktop environment, which preserves a traditional desktop experience
Focuses on out-of-the-box usability with pre-installed codecs and drivers
Consistently ranked as one of the most popular Linux distributions for desktop users

children.name: Feren OS
facts: Based on Linux Mint/Ubuntu with a focus on providing a familiar experience for Windows users
Features a customized KDE Plasma desktop environment with a Windows-like layout
Includes a theme manager for easy customization of the desktop appearance
Comes with a migration tool to help users transition from Windows

children.name: Zorin OS
facts: Designed to look and feel familiar to Windows users transitioning to Linux
Offers a unique 'Zorin Appearance' app to switch between different desktop layouts
Available in free Core edition and paid Pro edition with additional features

children.name: Elementary OS
facts: Known for its macOS-inspired design and user interface
Developed its own desktop environment called Pantheon
Focuses on a 'pay-what-you-want' funding model for sustainable open source development

children.name: Pop!_OS
facts: Developed by computer manufacturer System76
Optimized for graphics and compute workflows with specialized support for NVIDIA GPUs
Features unique window tiling and keyboard navigation system

children.name: Kubuntu
facts: Official Ubuntu flavor featuring the KDE Plasma desktop environment
Combines Ubuntu's infrastructure with KDE's applications and technologies
Offers a more customizable desktop experience compared to standard Ubuntu

children.name: Lubuntu
facts: Official Ubuntu flavor featuring the lightweight LXQt desktop environment
Designed for older computers with limited resources
Focuses on speed and energy-efficiency while maintaining usability

children.name: Xubuntu
facts: Official Ubuntu flavor featuring the Xfce desktop environment
Balances performance and features for a responsive system
Provides a traditional desktop experience with modern capabilities

children.name: Ubuntu MATE
facts: Official Ubuntu flavor featuring the MATE desktop environment
Continues the traditional GNOME 2 desktop experience
Focuses on usability and performance while maintaining familiarity

children.name: Ubuntu Budgie
facts: Official Ubuntu flavor featuring the Budgie desktop environment
Provides a modern, simplified desktop experience
Developed in collaboration with the Solus project

children.name: KDE Neon
facts: Combines an Ubuntu LTS base with the latest KDE Plasma desktop
Developed by KDE to showcase the latest KDE technologies
Not an official Ubuntu flavor but uses Ubuntu as its foundation

children.name: Ubuntu Studio
facts: Official Ubuntu flavor focused on multimedia creation
Includes specialized audio, video, and graphics applications
Features a low-latency kernel optimized for audio production

children.name: Ubuntu Kylin
facts: Official Ubuntu flavor developed in partnership with the Chinese government and CSIP
Designed specifically for Chinese users with localized software, fonts, and input methods
Ships with UKUI (Ubuntu Kylin User Interface), a custom desktop resembling Windows
Pre-installed with Chinese-specific apps like WPS Office, Sogou input method, and Youdao Dictionary

children.name: Bodhi Linux
facts: Named after the Buddhist term for 'awakening'; ships with the Moksha (formerly Enlightenment) desktop
Provides an extremely lightweight base and lets users install only what they need
The Moksha desktop uses very little RAM while still providing a visually distinctive experience
Comes in a legacy edition for computers as old as those with 500MHz processors

children.name: Linux Lite
facts: Explicitly designed to help Windows XP and Windows 7 users transition to Linux
Ships with a Windows-style Xfce layout and familiar applications like LibreOffice and VLC
Includes a 'Lite Welcome' app and 'Lite Software' tool to ease new users into Linux
Maintains long-term support releases aligned with Ubuntu LTS cycles

children.name: Peppermint OS
facts: Pioneered a hybrid desktop/cloud model using web apps integrated via SSB (Site Specific Browsers)
Extremely lightweight and fast, designed for older hardware with internet-centric workflows
After its original lead developer passed away in 2023, the community continued the project
Uses a customized XFCE/LXQt environment with ICE for seamless web app integration

children.name: Regolith Linux
facts: Combines Ubuntu's stability with the i3 tiling window manager for a keyboard-driven workflow
Designed for developers who want minimal mouse usage and maximum screen real estate
Uses Regolith's own configuration layer to make i3 more approachable for new tiling WM users
Inspired by the 'distraction-free' computing philosophy popular in developer communities

children.name: Nitrux
facts: Pioneered a desktop Linux distribution built around AppImage as the primary application format
Ships with NX Desktop, a custom KDE Plasma configuration with a macOS-inspired aesthetic
Adopts an immutable root filesystem model for increased system integrity
Developed by Urubu Computers; known for striking visual design and a forward-thinking philosophy

children.name: Pear OS
facts: Created by French developer David Tavares; heavily themed to resemble Apple's Mac OS X with a custom dock and GNOME 3 shell
Went through eight releases between 2011 and 2013, building a cult following among users who wanted macOS aesthetics on Linux
In 2014 Tavares sold the project to an undisclosed company and it vanished entirely — one of Linux's more mysterious endings
The concept was later independently revived as pearOS by a Romanian developer, rebuilt on Arch Linux with KDE Plasma

children.name: Ubuntu Unity
facts: Ubuntu flavor centered on the Unity desktop environment
Revives the Unity experience for modern Ubuntu releases

children.name: Ubuntu Cinnamon
facts: Ubuntu flavor that ships with the Cinnamon desktop
Combines Ubuntu packaging with a Cinnamon-centric workflow

children.name: Edubuntu
facts: Ubuntu flavor aimed at education and classroom use
Includes software and defaults intended for students and teachers

children.name: TUXEDO OS
facts: TUXEDO Computers operating system built on the latest Ubuntu LTS base
Uses KDE Plasma and ships with TUXEDO-specific hardware support and tools

children.name: Voyager 24.04 LTS
facts: Ubuntu-based edition built around GNOME and Xfce desktop experiences
Uses Ubuntu Noble as the base for the 24.04 LTS generation

children.name: Devuan
facts: Created as a fork of Debian to avoid the systemd init system
Named as a play on 'Debian' with 'dev' replacing 'deb' (developers taking back control)
Focuses on init system freedom and choice

children.name: MX Linux
facts: Collaboration between the antiX and former MEPIS Linux communities
Combines lightweight efficiency with elegant and user-friendly design
Consistently ranked as one of the most popular distributions on DistroWatch

children.name: Raspberry Pi OS
facts: Formerly known as Raspbian, optimized for Raspberry Pi hardware
Designed for educational purposes and IoT projects
Based on Debian with modifications for ARM architecture
Developed by the Raspberry Pi Foundation to support their hardware platform

children.name: KNOPPIX
facts: Created by Klaus Knopper; one of the first Linux distros designed to boot entirely from CD without installation
Pioneered the 'live distribution' concept that influenced dozens of later projects
Featured advanced hardware auto-detection that was revolutionary for its era
Used by system administrators worldwide as a rescue and diagnostic tool

children.name: KANOTIX
facts: Created by Jörg 'Kano' Schirottke as a fork of KNOPPIX focused on hardware detection and Debian Sid packages
Bridged the gap between KNOPPIX's legendary hardware detection and a full rolling-release Debian Sid desktop
Introduced many users to the concept of running Debian Unstable as a daily driver for the first time
The direct ancestor of sidux; when KANOTIX development slowed, the sidux project was born from its community

children.name: sidux
facts: Forked from KANOTIX with a singular focus: making Debian Sid safe and usable as a daily desktop
The name is a portmanteau of 'sid' (Debian's unstable branch codename) and 'ux' for Unix
Built a passionate community of power users who wanted bleeding-edge software with Debian's package depth
Development stalled and was effectively taken over and renamed to aptosid in 2010

children.name: aptosid
facts: Renamed from sidux after a community dispute; 'aptos' is Greek for 'apt' or 'fitting', referencing the APT package manager
Continued the mission of making Debian Sid accessible, with improved scripts for safe upgrades
Community tensions around openness and governance led a group of users to fork it as siduction in 2011
Ceased releasing ISOs around 2013, with the community having largely migrated to siduction

children.name: siduction
facts: Name is a pun on 'sid' (Debian Unstable) and 'seduction' — the seductive appeal of always-current software
Forked from aptosid specifically to create a more open, community-governed project that gives back to Debian upstream
One of the few actively maintained Debian Sid-based distributions, still releasing snapshots as of 2022+
Represents the end of a five-generation chain: Debian → KNOPPIX → KANOTIX → sidux → aptosid → siduction

children.name: antiX
facts: Named after the Greek island Antiparos; built for old and resource-constrained hardware
Uses SysVinit or runit instead of systemd, making it a popular choice for systemd skeptics
Serves as the base and co-parent of MX Linux alongside the former MEPIS community
Can run with as little as 256MB RAM and boots quickly on decade-old hardware

children.name: Trisquel
facts: One of very few distributions endorsed by the Free Software Foundation as containing zero proprietary software
Developed in Spain and named after a Celtic triskelion symbol
Richard Stallman's personal distribution of choice for daily use
Removes all non-free firmware and blobs from the Linux kernel

children.name: PureOS
facts: Developed by Purism, the company behind the Librem privacy-focused laptops and Librem 5 phone
FSF-endorsed as a fully free distribution with no proprietary blobs
Default OS for Purism hardware; also ships on the Librem 5 smartphone
Focuses on security, privacy, and digital rights by design

children.name: BackTrack
facts: Merged two older security distros — Whax and Auditor Security Collection — into one powerful toolkit
Became the go-to penetration testing distribution for a generation of security professionals
Discontinued in 2013 when Offensive Security rebuilt it from scratch as Kali Linux
Popularized security-focused Linux distributions and inspired many successors

children.name: Kali Linux
facts: Developed by Offensive Security as a successor to BackTrack
Includes hundreds of pre-installed tools for security testing and digital forensics
Used extensively by cybersecurity professionals and ethical hackers

children.name: Whonix
facts: Designed to run entirely inside virtual machines, isolating the user's identity from the OS
Uses two VMs: a 'Gateway' that routes all traffic through Tor, and a 'Workstation' for user activity
Even a compromised application cannot leak the user's real IP address due to the VM isolation model
Endorsed by privacy researchers and journalists working in hostile environments

children.name: LMDE
facts: Linux Mint Debian Edition — a version of Linux Mint built directly on Debian rather than Ubuntu
Serves as an 'insurance project' for Linux Mint in case Ubuntu ever becomes unsuitable as a base
Provides the familiar Linux Mint experience with a more upstream Debian foundation
Uses the Cinnamon desktop and ships with the same tools as regular Linux Mint

children.name: Snowlinux
facts: German distribution by Lars Torben Kremer, released in both a Debian-stable edition and a separate Ubuntu-based edition simultaneously
Offered multiple desktop environments including GNOME 2, KDE, MATE, Cinnamon, Xfce, and even Enlightenment E17
Unusual for maintaining two completely separate editions with different bases rather than choosing one upstream
Active only from 2011 to 2013; its server popularity overwhelmed its infrastructure, a victim of its own unexpected success

children.name: CrunchBang
facts: Created by Philip Newborough (corenominal) in the UK; early versions from 2008–2010 were Ubuntu-based before switching to Debian Stable in 2011
Built entirely around the Openbox window manager with Conky for desktop monitoring — no full desktop environment, just a razor-sharp minimal setup
Its name '#!' (hash-bang) is a Unix shebang — the two characters that begin executable scripts — a perfect emblem of its hacker aesthetic
Newborough discontinued it in 2015 saying users would be better off with vanilla Debian; the community emphatically disagreed, spawning multiple successors

children.name: BunsenLabs
facts: Community-organized successor to CrunchBang, started by former CrunchBang forum moderators within days of Newborough's discontinuation announcement
CrunchBang's own domain redirected to BunsenLabs for two weeks in 2015, effectively passing the torch publicly
Named after Robert Bunsen, the chemist — continuing CrunchBang's tradition of scientific/elemental release codenames
Still actively maintained as of 2025, tracking Debian Stable releases; one of the longest-lived community rescue projects in Linux history

children.name: CrunchBang++
facts: Parallel community successor to CrunchBang launched in April 2015, hewing more closely to the original's stark monochrome aesthetic than BunsenLabs
Name written as '#!++' — the original hash-bang plus a C++ increment operator — a double nod to its Unix and programming roots
Keeps AbiWord and Gnumeric rather than upgrading to heavier apps, preserving CrunchBang's philosophy of 'use the lightest tool that works'
Still actively released as of 2025, tracking Debian Stable; both CB++ and BunsenLabs continue to coexist as distinct takes on the same legacy

children.name: Q4OS
facts: Desktop-oriented operating system based on Debian Linux
Focuses on getting a productive, low-overhead system quickly

children.name: SparkyLinux
facts: Fast and lightweight distribution based on Debian GNU/Linux
Offers stable and semi-rolling editions with a variety of desktop choices

children.name: SolydXK
facts: Community Linux distribution derived from Debian
Focused on a polished desktop experience with KDE and Xfce variants

children.name: Red Hat Linux
facts: One of the first commercially successful Linux companies
Evolved into Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL), a paid enterprise distribution with long-term support
Red Hat was acquired by IBM in 2019 for $34 billion, one of the largest software acquisitions in history

children.name: Red Hat Enterprise Linux
facts: Commercial, enterprise-grade Linux distribution with professional support
Features long lifecycles and stability for business environments
Serves as the foundation for many other enterprise Linux distributions

children.name: Fedora
facts: Serves as the upstream testing ground for Red Hat Enterprise Linux
Known for adopting new technologies early, like Wayland and systemd
Sponsored by Red Hat but developed by a community of volunteers
Releases new versions approximately every 6 months

children.name: Bazzite
facts: Built on Fedora Atomic (formerly Silverblue) with a focus on gaming as a SteamOS alternative for PCs
Ships with the latest drivers, Wayland, HDR support, and gaming optimizations out of the box
Uses rpm-ostree for atomic system updates that can be rolled back if something breaks
Rapidly gained popularity as an alternative to Windows for gaming on regular PC hardware

children.name: Fedora Silverblue
facts: Fedora's flagship immutable desktop variant using rpm-ostree for atomic OS updates
Applications are installed via Flatpak, keeping the base OS read-only and consistent
Inspired the broader 'immutable Linux desktop' movement including uBlue and Bazzite
Designed for developers who want a stable, reproducible base while experimenting with containers

children.name: Fedora Workstation
facts: Fedora’s flagship desktop edition, designed for laptops and desktop computers
Ships with GNOME and a curated set of tools for developers and makers

children.name: Fedora Server
facts: Official Fedora server distribution aimed at datacenter and infrastructure workloads
Offers a flexible server base with modern platform technologies

children.name: Fedora IoT
facts: Fedora edition focused on IoT and embedded workloads
Uses image-based and container-friendly workflows for device deployments

children.name: Fedora Kinoite
facts: Immutable Fedora desktop edition centered on KDE Plasma
Shares the atomic desktop model with Fedora Silverblue while targeting KDE users

children.name: CentOS
facts: Originally a community rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux source code
In 2020, shifted to CentOS Stream, which serves as the upstream for RHEL rather than a downstream clone
The original CentOS project was acquired by Red Hat in 2014

children.name: CentOS Stream
facts: Rolling preview of Red Hat Enterprise Linux with a community focus
Serves as the upstream development platform for future RHEL releases
Provides a middle ground between Fedora and RHEL in terms of stability and cutting-edge features

children.name: Rocky Linux
facts: Created by Gregory Kurtzer, the original founder of CentOS
Developed in response to the CentOS project's shift to CentOS Stream
Aims to be a 100% bug-for-bug compatible with Red Hat Enterprise Linux

children.name: AlmaLinux
facts: Developed by CloudLinux as a response to the CentOS Stream shift
Designed as a stable, production-ready drop-in replacement for CentOS
Governed by a non-profit organization with community involvement

children.name: Scientific Linux
facts: Joint project between CERN and Fermilab to provide a stable platform for high-energy physics research
A binary-compatible rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux with scientific software pre-installed
Used on thousands of machines at particle accelerator facilities worldwide
Discontinued in 2019 as Rocky Linux and AlmaLinux emerged as successors after CentOS's pivot

children.name: Oracle Linux
facts: Oracle's binary-compatible rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux, offered free to download
Includes Oracle's 'Unbreakable Enterprise Kernel' (UEK), a heavily patched alternative kernel
Controversially positioned as a cheaper alternative to RHEL with Oracle support contracts
Widely used in Oracle Database environments and Oracle Cloud Infrastructure

children.name: Nobara Linux
facts: Fedora-based distribution tuned for gaming and desktop use
Maintained by GloriousEggroll to reduce post-install tweaking

children.name: Ultramarine Linux
facts: Fedora-based distribution focused on a ready-to-use desktop experience
Ships with opinionated defaults and a community-oriented workflow

children.name: Bluefin
facts: Universal Blue desktop image built on Fedora
Targets a polished workstation experience with immutable foundations

children.name: Aurora
facts: Universal Blue desktop image built on Fedora
Targets developer workflows while keeping the same immutable base model

children.name: Gentoo Linux
facts: Known for its source-based package management system called Portage
Allows users to optimize software for their specific hardware
Named after the fast-swimming Gentoo penguin species
Emphasizes user choice and control over the system

children.name: Calculate Linux
facts: Gentoo-based distribution developed in Russia
Designed for easy installation and use in corporate environments
Provides ready-to-use desktop and server solutions

children.name: Funtoo Linux
facts: Created by Daniel Robbins, the original founder of Gentoo
Focuses on performance improvements and new features
Uses Git for its Portage tree instead of rsync

children.name: Sabayon Linux
facts: Aims to provide an easy-to-use, out-of-the-box Gentoo experience
Offers binary package support for faster installation
Focuses on multimedia and gaming support

children.name: ChromeOS
facts: Google's operating system for Chromebooks, built on a Gentoo Linux foundation
By sheer number of active devices, ChromeOS may be the most widely deployed Linux-based desktop OS
Designed around the Chrome browser with all apps running as web apps or Android apps
ChromiumOS is the open-source version; ChromeOS adds Google's proprietary services on top

children.name: Pentoo
facts: Security-focused Gentoo-based live distribution for penetration testing
One of the few security distributions not based on Debian, taking advantage of Gentoo's optimized builds
Ships as a live CD/USB that can also be installed to disk
Focused on WiFi auditing, exploits, and forensics with a Gentoo philosophy of optimization

children.name: Redcore Linux
facts: Gentoo-based distribution that adds binary packages for convenience
Aims to make the Gentoo base more approachable for desktop users

children.name: NixOS
facts: Uses the Nix package manager with a purely functional deployment model
Allows for reliable system upgrades and rollbacks
Enables multiple versions of packages to coexist on the same system
Configuration is described in a declarative language

children.name: home-manager
facts: Not a full distribution but a tool to manage user environments using Nix
Allows declarative configuration of user dotfiles and packages
Can be used on any Linux distribution with Nix installed

children.name: Guix System
facts: GNU's take on functional/declarative package management; a spiritual sibling to NixOS
Uses GNU Guile (a Scheme dialect) for system configuration instead of NixOS's Nix language
FSF-endorsed as a fully free distribution with no proprietary software or firmware
Supports transactional upgrades, rollbacks, and per-user package environments like Nix

children.name: Void Linux
facts: Independent distribution not based on any other Linux distribution
Offers choice between musl libc and glibc
Uses the XBPS package manager and runit init system instead of systemd
Features a rolling release model with a focus on stability

children.name: Alpine Linux
facts: Designed to be small, simple, and secure
Uses musl libc and busybox instead of GNU components
Extremely popular as a base for Docker containers
Uses OpenRC as its init system instead of systemd

children.name: postmarketOS
facts: Aims to give smartphones a longer life by bringing Linux to them
Based on Alpine Linux with modifications for mobile devices
Supports a wide range of old and new mobile devices

children.name: GL.iNet OpenWrt
facts: GL.iNet produces travel routers running OpenWrt, a Linux distribution derived from Alpine concepts for embedded networking
OpenWrt itself originated as a Linksys WRT54G firmware hack in 2004, becoming a full embedded Linux project
Runs on devices with as little as 4MB flash and 16MB RAM
Powers millions of home routers, access points, and embedded networking devices worldwide

children.name: Arch Linux
facts: Famous for its 'Keep It Simple, Stupid' (KISS) philosophy and minimalist approach
Uses a rolling release model where packages are continuously updated rather than in major version releases
Designed for users who want full control over their system configuration

children.name: Manjaro
facts: Makes Arch Linux more accessible with a user-friendly installer and pre-configured desktop environments
Holds packages for additional testing before release, unlike Arch's immediate updates

children.name: EndeavourOS
facts: Created as a spiritual successor to Antergos (another user-friendly Arch-based distro)
Aims to be closer to vanilla Arch while still providing an easy installation process

children.name: ArcoLinux
facts: Educational Arch-based distribution with multiple desktop options
Focuses on teaching users about Arch Linux through a learning path
Offers several editions with different levels of pre-configuration

children.name: Garuda Linux
facts: Focuses on performance, gaming, and visual appeal
Ships with Btrfs filesystem and advanced snapshots by default
Offers several desktop environments and a rolling release model

children.name: BlackArch
facts: Provides a massive repository of over 2,800 security and penetration testing tools on top of Arch
Can be installed as a standalone distro or added as a repository overlay to an existing Arch install
Maintained by a community of security researchers and ethical hackers
Known for having one of the most comprehensive collections of security tools of any distribution

children.name: SteamOS 3
facts: Valve's third-generation SteamOS, rebuilt on Arch Linux for the Steam Deck handheld console
Uses an A/B partition scheme for atomic updates that can be rolled back instantly
Runs Windows games via Proton, a compatibility layer built on Wine and DXVK
SteamOS 1 and 2 were Debian-based; the move to Arch in v3 was a significant architectural shift

children.name: CachyOS
facts: Arch-based distribution focused on extreme performance through custom-compiled packages
Offers several optimized kernels including the CachyOS kernel with BORE and other schedulers
Uses x86-64-v3 and x86-64-v4 instruction sets for measurably faster binaries on modern CPUs
Popular in the Linux gaming community for competitive performance against Windows

children.name: pearOS
facts: A spiritual revival of the original Pear OS concept, independently created by Romanian developer Alexandru Bălan
Unlike the Ubuntu-based original, pearOS is built on Arch Linux with a heavily customized KDE Plasma desktop mimicking macOS
Features a working global menu bar, a pear logo replacing the Apple menu, and a rolling-release model via Arch
Demonstrates how a concept — macOS aesthetics on Linux — can independently re-emerge years later on a completely different technical foundation

children.name: OBRevenge
facts: Created by Jody James; 'OB' stands for Openbox, the ultra-lightweight window manager at the distro's core
Stood out among Arch-based distros by using the full AUR unlike Manjaro, while still offering an easy graphical installer via Calamares
Shipped with custom in-house tools for graphics drivers, GRUB configuration, codec installation, and panel switching
Development ended in 2017 when Jody pivoted to the Zen Installer project, a universal Arch installer that outlived OBRevenge itself

children.name: ArchLabs
facts: Arch-based minimalist distribution centered on Openbox and later i3wm, inspired by the CrunchBang aesthetic applied to Arch
Developed with community input from OBRevenge circles, including contribution from Jody James of OBRevenge
Known for its striking dark theming and the 'al-hello' post-install configuration script for customizing the environment
Represents the philosophical cross-pollination between the CrunchBang/Openbox minimalist world and the Arch rolling-release world

children.name: Artix Linux
facts: Arch-derived distribution that avoids systemd
Offers OpenRC and runit init choices for users who want alternatives to systemd

children.name: Parabola GNU/Linux-libre
facts: Arch-based distribution that removes nonfree software
Recognized by the Free Software Foundation as a fully free system

children.name: Archcraft
facts: Arch-based distribution focused on a polished desktop experience
Emphasizes lightweight window managers and pre-tuned visuals

children.name: RebornOS
facts: Arch-based rolling release distribution with a large selection of desktops and window managers
Designed to make Arch Linux more approachable without hiding its core flexibility

children.name: blendOS
facts: Arch Linux variant that is immutable, declarative, and atomic
Supports multiple desktop tracks and containerized distro integration

children.name: ChimeraOS
facts: Gaming-focused operating system built to provide a couch gaming experience
Boots directly into a controller-first Steam Big Picture style interface

children.name: ALCI
facts: Arch Linux Calamares Installer project aimed at simplifying Arch installation
Provides graphical installation workflows with Arch-like flexibility

children.name: Puppy Linux
facts: Designed to run entirely in RAM for speed and responsiveness
Can run from USB drives or CD-ROMs without installation
Extremely small footprint, often under 300MB
Includes a variety of applications despite its small size

children.name: Porteus
facts: Modular Slackware-based live distribution
Designed to be fast, portable, and lightweight
Uses a modular approach allowing easy customization
Can be booted from various media including USB, CD, or hard drive

children.name: Tiny Core Linux
facts: One of the smallest Linux distributions with a graphical interface
Core system is only about 16MB in size
Based on a modular design where users add only what they need
Designed to run entirely in RAM for speed and minimal disk usage

children.name: Tails
facts: The Amnesic Incognito Live System designed for privacy and anonymity
Routes all internet traffic through the Tor network
Leaves no digital footprint on the host computer
Designed to be booted as a live system from USB or DVD

children.name: Qubes OS
facts: Security-focused desktop operating system that uses virtualization for compartmentalization
Isolates different activities in separate virtual machines (qubes)
Endorsed by privacy advocates including Edward Snowden
Implements the principle of security by isolation

children.name: Hannah Montana Linux
facts: A real, functional Ubuntu-based distribution with a Hannah Montana theme — pink desktop, Hannah wallpapers, and custom icons
Created as a whimsical project that became a beloved internet meme in Linux communities
Demonstrated that even frivolous theming projects could attract community attention and real users
Now preserved as a piece of Linux culture history; the ISO is still circulated nostalgically

children.name: Linux From Scratch
facts: Not a distribution but a book and project teaching users to build a Linux system entirely from source code
Created by Gerard Beekmans as a learning exercise; the 'LFS Book' has been continuously updated since 1999
Completing LFS gives deep understanding of every component that makes up a Linux system
Beyond LFS (BLFS) extends the project with a full desktop and application stack built from source

children.name: SteamOS 1
facts: Valve's first SteamOS, Debian-based, designed for living-room gaming on Steam Machines
Launched alongside Valve's Steam Machine hardware initiative which was ultimately discontinued
Ran Steam in Big Picture mode by default with a custom compositor for TV use
Demonstrated Valve's long-term commitment to Linux gaming despite the hardware line's commercial failure

children.name: KaOS
facts: Independent distribution focused on Qt and KDE software
Built from scratch rather than derived from another general-purpose distro

children.name: Bedrock Linux
facts: Meta distribution that lets users mix and match components from multiple Linux distributions
Designed to integrate strata from different distros into one cohesive system
